coredata.dc:title: Static interaction between colloidal carbon nano-dots and aniline: A novel platform for ultrasensitive detection of aniline in aqueous media
coredata.dc:creator: Bakier, Y.M.
Ghali, M.
Elkun, A.
Beltagi, A.M.
Zahra, W.K.
coredata.dc:description: Aniline has been classified as a probable human carcinogen which can cause severe respiratory tract irritation and congestion to humans. Here, we propose using ultra-small (1.8 nm) colloidal nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots (N-CDs), that is green synthesized at a low temperature of only 100 °C from ultrasonic-processed water-soluble folic acid, as a novel turn-off fluorescent chemo-sensor for ultrasensitive detection of aniline in aqueous medium. It was found that the Stern-Volmer relation describes very well the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by aniline and confirms a static quenching mechanism between them. Compared to other techniques, our N-CDs based optical detection method, is the simplest and the most ultrasensitive method for aniline liquid detection, reaching an exceptional low detection limit of 3.57 nM (0.332 ppb). The proposed method was successfully applied for determination of aniline in a tap water sample without significant interferences from the matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Luminescence
Aniline
Chemo-sensor
Detection limit

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and fundamental properties of N-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Academy of Scientific Research and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the hydrothermal synthesis process of N-CDs from ultrasonically processed folic acid at temperature 100 °C.

(a) TEM and (b) HR-TEM images of the synthesized N-CDs. The insets of (a) and (b) are histograms of the dots distributions and the lattice spacing of the crystalline dots, with d = 0.21 nm, respectively.
(a) XRD pattern of the synthesized N-CDs. (b) XPS of full survey. (c-e) high resolution XPS of O1 s, N1 s and C1 s, respectively. (f) Raman spectrum of N-CDs where the D and G bands are indicated.

(a) FTIR spectra of FA and N-CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of aniline (green line), N-CDs (red line) and the N-CDs/aniline system (black line). Fig.4b/B shows effect of aniline on the absorption spectra of N-CDs, where the difference absorption spectroscopy between the spectra of [N-CDs/Aniline] system and aniline alone (black curve in Fig.4b/B, at the same concentrations, are measured and compared with the N-CDs absorption spectrum alone (red curve in Fig.4b/B).

(a) A photo of the colloidal N-CDs under ambient and UV light. (b) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs under different photoexcitation wavelengths. The inset is the fluorescence excitation spectrum detected at λ = 455 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of aniline in the range 1.96 × 10−8 -8.97 × 10-8 M. (b) The Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching I0/I of N-CDs by aniline in water.

A histogram data shows the selectivity of N-CDs for aniline: The blue columns represent (I/I0) of N-CDs in the presence of several other (a) organic (b) inorganic liquids usually present as cationic and ionic in drinking water. The green columns represent (I/I0) of N-CDs in the presence of each other liquid and aniline.
Fluorescence stability of the N-CDs in aqueous solution as measured at different times from 0 to 7 days. The inset shows negligible variation of the fluorescence intensity with time.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of aniline in tap water in the range 1.96 × 10−8 -8.97 × 10-8 M. (b) The Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching (I0/I-1) of N-CDs by aniline at different concentrations in tap water.
Fluorescence decay data of N-CDs in the absence and presence of different concentration of aniline in the range 1.96 × 10−8-8.97 × 10−8 M.
(a) zeta potential of the investigated N-CDs, (b) Representation of the mechanism of interaction between aniline and N-CDs.
The plot of I0/(I0- I) as a function of 1/[Aniline] for calculation of the fraction of the initial fluorescence accessible to aniline in N-CDs/aniline complex, f.
Calibration parameters of our aniline liquid chemo-sensor.
Aniline detection using different methods in the literature.

row.entry: The modified electrode of Ce-PbO2 as an analytical sensor
Carbon dot-silica aerogel composite treated with the gelatin of tetraethyl orthosilicate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results of precision and accuracy using standard addition method for our N-CDs sensor as applied in tap water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Static interaction between colloidal carbon nano-dots and aniline: A novel platform for ultrasensitive detection of aniline in aqueous media

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Energy Materials and Applied Computational Mathematics, Basic and Applied Science Institute
Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aniline has been classified as a probable human carcinogen which can cause severe respiratory tract irritation and congestion to humans. Here, we propose using ultra-small (1.8 nm) colloidal nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots (N-CDs), that is green synthesized at a low temperature of only 100 °C from ultrasonic-processed water-soluble folic acid, as a novel turn-off fluorescent chemo-sensor for ultrasensitive detection of aniline in aqueous medium. It was found that the Stern-Volmer relation describes very well the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by aniline and confirms a static quenching mechanism between them. Compared to other techniques, our N-CDs based optical detection method, is the simplest and the most ultrasensitive method for aniline liquid detection, reaching an exceptional low detection limit of 3.57 nM (0.332 ppb). The proposed method was successfully applied for determination of aniline in a tap water sample without significant interferences from the matrix.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the progress of nanomaterials and particularly semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), into potential fluorescent investigations in sensing processes, has appealed substantial attention because of their benefits of high efficiency, stability, and selectivity [1]. However, semiconductor QDs have definite restraints such as high poisonousness, several steps, and high cost of their fabrication. Recently, carbon nano-dots (CDs) have demonstrated more preference over semiconductor QDs for utilization in optical sensors due to their chemical inertness, biocompatibility, and nonpoisonous besides the facile and the low cost of their synthesis [2]. Moreover, doping CDs with heteroatoms such as O, N or S is essential for increasing their fluorescence quantum yield (QY) [3]. Conversely, aniline is a crucial chemical intermediate, which is widely used in chemical industrial and pharmacological productions. Yet, the toxicity of aniline is high, where a tiny quantity even with a concentration in the micromolar scale can cause harmful and dangerous effects to humans such as upper respiratory tract irritation [4]. Therefore, aniline is classified as carcinogenic to humans, particularly that aniline can be used in many industrial sources, and may be found in various locations e.g., surface water. Consequently, for the quick warning, it is crucial to develop potential sensors that can detect low concentrations of aniline. Several techniques have been used for aniline detection, such as solid-phase micro-extraction [5], and the Ce-PbO2 modified electrode [6]. However, these techniques usually require costly stationary instruments and complex networks. Also, fluorescence-based sensors have verified the easiest and cheap operation approaches for amines sensing [7,8]. Yet, a few works have been reported for aniline detection using fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, most materials used for this purpose are either expensive and/or have potential toxicity effects [9–12] or their synthesis procedures are complicated and lengthy [13–16]. Besides, the detection of aniline, based on these materials, is usually limited to concentrations in the micromolar scale [6]. On the other hand, the production of bright nitrogen-doped carbon dots from folic acid (FA) has been recently reported by several groups [2,17]. For example, Liu et al. [17], showed that FA can be carbonized by hydrothermal method in the high synthesis temperature range, between 180–240 °C, without ultrasonic processing of FA. They found that the luminescence quantum yield of the synthesized dots increases substantially from 9% to 79.9 % when the synthesis temperature approaches the melting point of FA (240 °C). Also, Ma et al., [18] reported on large size (50 nm) carbon dot-silica aerogel composite treated with the gelatin of tetraethyl orthosilicate at high temperature and used them for prelaminar sensing of aniline gas. The results showed relatively low sensitivity (77.6 ppm) of the sensor. Here, we introduce a small-sized (1.8 nm) nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) as the most sensitive, selective, and environmentally friendly chemo-sensor for aniline liquid detection in aqueous medium. Our N-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal of ultrasonically processed water-soluble folic acid, which plays a dual role in the synthesis process as a source of carbon and nitrogen. In contrary to other works in literature, our synthesis of N-CDs is a one-step low-temperature approach operating at only 100 °C. The N-CDs sensor showed the highest sensitivity of aniline liquid detection with limit of detection (LOD) 3.57 nM (0.332 ppb), which is the smallest value achieved up to now by any means. Finally, the performance of our N-CDs sensor was successfully tested using real tap water sample with good results. This work is important for developing an easy, fast and eco-friendly fluorescence-based nano-sensor for use in toxic aniline monitoring for combating natural contamination.

Aniline was obtained from El-Gomhouria, Egypt, and was used after puriﬁcation by vacuum distillation. Folic acid was received with purity of 99.9 % (Acros organics, Belgium). Toluene (99.8 %), chloroform (99.8 %), DMSO (99.7 %), DMF (99.8 %) and acetone (99.6 %) were purchased from Acros Organics, Belgium. Diphenylamine (98 %) from Laboratory Rasayan, India, while diphenylamine and pyridine (99.9 %) from Fisher Chemical, Loughborough. 1000 mg L−1 stock solutions of each of K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, SO4 2− and HCO3 − ions, in addition to 100 mg L−1 stock solutions of each of Fe2+ and Cu2+ ions were all prepared in de-ionized water from salts of KCl, NaHCO3, MgSO4 and CaCl2, CuCl2 and FeCl2 (Merck). They were used in the interference’s studies.

High-Resolution TEM(JEOL2100 F) is used for surface and structural investigation of the synthesized N-CDs. X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were obtained using Shimadzu XRD6100. FTIR spectra are recorded by Bruker Vertex 70. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) curves were executed using K-ALPHA. Dispersive Raman microscope (Sunterra, Bruker) was used to record the Raman spectra at wavelength 532 nm. The optical absorption and fluorescence spectra are documented using U-3900 spectrophotometer and F-2700 spectrophotometer, respectively. The steady-state fluorescence measurements were conducted by adding 1 mL of N-CDs in 1 cm path length rectangular quartz cuvette, where the excitation and the detection slits were 5 nm. The photomultiplier of the spectrofluorometer was set at operating voltage of 400 V. During the fluorescence measurements, and to establish the equilibration, each spectrum was recorded after 5 min of the mixing with the analytics. Each spectrum was taken for 3 times and the average of the fluorescence intensity (FL) was recorded and considering for the determination of the I0/I ratio. Where I0 and I are the FL in the absence and presence of aniline, respectively. The Fluorescence decay spectra were measured via nanosecond time-correlated single-photon-counting (Horiba 3000U).

To check the aniline sensing ability of the as-synthesized N-CDs in environmental samples, a recovery test was carried out using tap water collected from the industrial city of New Borg Al-Arab (Alexandria, Egypt). It should be noted that the city includes several dozens of food processing factories with surface rather than ground water supply. Next, N-CDs were added (48 nM) to the tap water, then different concentrations of aniline were spiked with the dots. Finally, the spiked tap water sample was analyzed using the standard addition method.

The carbonization procedure, as shown Fig.1 , is applied for FA as the individual ingredient to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon nano-dots. A 30 mg of (FA) was liquefied in 45 mL of ultra-pure water. And the FA liquid was heated in an 80 mL Teflon-based autoclave for 6 h at 100 °C in a temperature-regulated thermal oven. For the synthesis process, two points should be explicitly mentioned: (i) the FA aqueous solution was ultra-sonicated continuously for 30 min using high power probe sonicator under continuous illumination by white light source (50 W).

We aimed from this step to facilitate breaking down the hydrogen bonds in the FA, because aqueous solutions of FA are typically heat sensitive and can decompose rapidly in the presence of light [19]. This step was necessarily for a complete dissolving of FA before loading it into the autoclave, (ii) the sonicated FA solution filled about 60 % of the total volume of the autoclave, and thus under this condition a very high hydrostatic pressure is expected inside the autoclave. The latter facilitates, along with the ultrasonic processing, the carbonization of FA. The above-mentioned experimental conditions are significantly different from that reported in literature, for the synthesis of carbon dots from folic acid, where high synthesis temperature is usually reported when no ultrasonic processing of FA is made [17]. The synthesized N-CDs were purified by 0.22 μm glass filter, then centrifuged at a high speed of 15,000 rpm to remove precipitates and finally the supernatant was dialyzed against purified water using a dialysis membrane with molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 24 h. The obtained N-CDs were stored at 4 °C temperature for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and fundamental properties of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a presents TEM data of the prepared N-CDs, where the nano-dots show a nearly monodispersed and semi-circular shape particles with average size 1.8 nm, as determined from the statistical analysis of more than 90 dots (see Fig. 2a, inset). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image displays fringe lines, with an interatomic crystalline lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, that match well with the graphitic configuration (Fig.2b) [3]. Fig. 3 a shows the XRD measurements of the synthesized N-CDs and reveals the 002 crystallographic plane at 2θ = 25°, with the d-interlayer spacing of 0.38 nm which is lengthier than that of graphite structure. The deviation in the extracted information of d-spacing, as obtained from XRD and that of HRTEM, indicate that our N-CDs have some lattice distortions owing to the existence of nitrogen atoms and surface functional groups, as will be discussed below. The elemental compositional analysis of the synthesized N-CDs, as determined from a full survey of the XPS spectrum, exposes that the obtained dots hold carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen origins with percentages of 74.18 %, 17.97 %, and 7.84 % wt., respectively (see Fig.3b).

These data are represented by three typical peaks: N 1s (398.97 eV), O 1s (531.97 eV) and C 1s (284.38 eV). We find from Fig. 3c that the high-resolution spectra of C 1s band could be deconvoluted into three bands at 287.35, 285.69, and 284.15 eV, representing the C 1s states in CO, CN/CO and, CC/CC respectively.

The O 1s band holds three peaks at 532.72, 531.39, and 530.68 eV for CO, COH/C-O-C, and CO respectively (Fig.3d). The N 1s spectrum exhibits a peak at 398.97 eV, expressing pyridinic N (Fig.3e). These results suggest the surface of the synthesized N-CDs to be covered by several functional groups e.g., −COOH, −OH, and NH groups. Figure 3f shows Raman spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs and confirms the formation of the two major bands of CDs at about 1417 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1, which are assigned to the characteristic d-band (1340−1400 cm−1) and the G-band (1570−1700 cm−1) of carbon nanostructures, respectively [20,21]. The ID/IG ratio was found to be 2.3, thus reflecting the existence of various defects in the core of the carbon nano-dots. Such high defect structure can be generally certified to the small size of our N-CDs.

Fig. 4 a shows the FTIR spectra of the synthesized N-CDs and FA. The broad peak at 3460 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group OH. The latter, reflects the vast number of the carboxyl groups on the N-CDs surface, while the band at 3387 cm−1 is linked to the stretching vibration of the NH group [17]. The band at 2080 cm−1 is publicized to CC, and the peak at 1640 cm−1 is ascribed to stretching vibrations of carboxyl groups CO. Moreover, CN bonds appear in the FTIR spectra at 1700 cm-1 and 2100 cm-1. For the N-CDs/aniline system, the FTIR spectrum, shown at the top of Fig.4a, indicates no change in the surface functional groups, however, the peaks are slightly shifted to the lower wavenumber and their intensities became less and broad.

Figs. 4b shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CDs which exhibit two apparent absorption bands at around 280 nm and 350 nm. The observed absorption band at ∼ 280 nm is generally attributed to the π→π* transition of the aromatic sp2 carbons within the dots’core and the absorption of FA, while the band at 350 nm

is ascribed to n→π* transitions [22,23]. Fig. 4b also displays the UV–vis absorption spectra of aniline and the N-CDs/aniline system. The absorption spectrum of aniline indicates two peaks at 229 nm and 280 nm which are attributed to π -π* conversion. It is noted that the spectra of N-CDs/aniline system reserve the spectroscopic features of aniline and N-CDs, while after adding aniline to the N-CDs, the absorption spectrum is found to be slightly blue shifted by ∼ 2 nm. In addition, with the naked eyes, under UV exposure, the N-CDs show bright blue fluorescence, which indicates that the N-CDs are well-dispersed in aqueous solution (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared N-CDs was calculated via standard reference in water according to the following equation [4]:
(2)
Q Y
N - C D s
=
Q Y
T
d I n t
d a b s
N - C D s
d I n t
d a b s
T
r
N - C D s
2
/
r T 2
where QYN-CDs is the quantum yield of N-CDs while QYT is the quantum yield of the reference, Int. represented the integrated area of the fluorescence curves, r is the refractive index of the solvent (water), and abs. is the corresponding absorbance. The QY of the synthesized N-CDs was calculated to be 47.24 % based on Eq. 2. This high QY, at the low synthesis temperature of only 100 °C, is owing to the special synthesis procedure which we used as described in Fig. 1, compared to other methods in literature, and to the existence of nitrogen and the surface defects on the nano-dots. The origin of the fluorescence of the N-CDs most likely due to the nitrogen-bonded aromatic carbon rings on the exterior and/or inside the dots. Also, under different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were examined, as shown in Fig. 5b, and demonstrate an excitation dependent behavior. The latter may be related to optical excitation of various surface states impurities close to the Fermi level of N-CDs, which is connected with the size and the surface chemical groups [5]. We also notice from Fig. 5a, that the fluorescence band shape is approximately symmetric and had a linewidth of ∼ 67.6 nm, which is expressively less than that of most reported carbon dots with blue emission (∼ 100 nm). This implies the mono-dispersity of our N-CDs and confirms what was observed in the TEM image. The strongest fluorescence intensity was observed at λ = 455 nm under photoexcitation ∼ 360 nm, as evidenced from the excitation spectrum of the synthesized N-CDs (see Fig.5b).

Different concentrations of aniline liquid in the range 1.96 × 10−8-8.97 × 10−8 M are added to the synthesized N-CDs for fluorescence detection of aniline. As seen in Fig.6 a, we observe a decrease in the N-CDs fluorescence intensity with increasing aniline concentration, which proves that aniline quenches significantly the fluorescence of N-CDs. Under photoexcitation at λ = 360 nm, quenching of the blue light fluorescence of the synthesized N-CDs can be seen with the naked eyes (see inset of Fig.6a). In order to explore the mechanism behind the observed fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence data are evaluated by the Stern–Volmer relation:
I o
I
= 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ] where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of the N-CDs with and without aniline, respectively, Ksv is the quenching constant while Q is the aniline concentration. As seen in Fig.6b, the plot of I0/I versus Q shows an excellent linear plot (R2 = 0.998), where Ksv is calculated and found to be 1.823 × 106 M-1. This result suggests that our N-CDs can be used for sensing aniline liquid. From the relation L O D =
3
S d
s l o p e
, where Sd = 0.00216 is the standard deviation of the intercept, taking into consideration the high signal-to-noise ratio of the spectra, the limit of detection (LOD) of aniline liquid was calculated and found to be 3.75 nM. (see calibration data in Table 1 ).

This value is the smallest one obtained so far for aniline liquid detection and demonstrate that N-CDs is the most sensitive fluorescence detector of aniline compared to other methods in literature (see Table 2 ). Moreover, as shown in Table 1, the intercept magnitude of the calibration line is found to 0.977 which is very close to 1, and thus imply that the observed fluorescence quenching is mainly due to the presence of aniline with minimal contributions from other interference quenchers (e.g., water). We emphasize that the change in the fluorescence intensity due to change in the dots concentration, within the linear range of our sensor, is less than 1% (which is far lower than that observed due to aniline).

We also studied the selectivity of aniline among other solvents including acetone, chloroform, DMF, DMSO, toluene and with other water-soluble amines (Fig.7 a). As shown in Fig.7a, we find that aniline presents stronger N-CDs fluorescence quenching than other investigated chemicals. These results demonstrate that our N-CDs proves high selectivity for aniline detection. Moreover, the developed N-CDs sensor was used for the determination of 10 ppb aniline in the presence of 100 ppm of each of Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+, Cl−, HCO3 − and SO4 2− ions without interferences from these species (Fig.7b). These ions are the major cations and anions normally found in natural water samples. The presence of some other metal ions, which are usually found in water samples in trace concentration levels, as Cu2+ and Fe2+ were also investigated. As shown in Fig.7b, our sensor is highly sensitive for aniline than the other cations or anions. These data reveal that our method is interference-free towards many organic and inorganic species and the developed N-CDs sensor can be applied for ultra-trace determination of aniline in water samples.

On the other hand, as the response rate of any sensor is crucial for a high and fast sensitivity, therefore a time scan of the N-CDs fluorescence intensity was also executed at excitation wavelength of 360 nm for a week. It was seen from Fig. 8 that the fluorescence intensity remained stable throughout the time scan.

Small amounts of aniline may be found in some foods, such as corn, grains, rhubarb, apples and beans. It has been detected in drinking water and has also been found in surface water [25]. Therefore, a sensitive and selective sensor is specifically needed for the sensing of aniline in water samples. A tap water sample from our lab. was taken and filtered through 0.45 μm milli-pore filter (Gelman, Germany) and the water sample was used without further treatment. Our N-CDs nanoprobe method was applied for determination of aniline in the tap water sample (Fig. 9 a). The concentration of aniline was estimated, using both calibration curve and the multiple standard addition methods (Fig. 9b), to found 60 nM (5.59 ppb). Slope values of both methods were close to each other confirming again the absence of matrix effects in the real sample. The accuracy of our N-CDs sensor was examined by mean percentage recovery (R%) and relative error (RE%) calculation [26].

The precision was also assessed from the relative standard deviation (RSD %) of the mean recovery (see Table 3 ). Moreover, we examined the repeatability and the reproducibility of our sensor. The repeatability is defined, according to ICH guidelines [26], as the replicated measurements within the day of different analytics concentrations in the same laboratory by the same analyst and using the same equipment. While reproducibility is the measurements of different analytics concentrations by different analysts at different elapsed time within lab-variations [26]. In the present work, two different aniline concentrations of 19.608 nM and 74.074 nM were repeatedly measured, while for the reproducibility test the measurements were conducted after two days from the fresh examinations, for 3 days over a week by two different analysts. All results were highly reproducible as indicated from mean R%, RE% and RSD%. The detection efficiency of aniline in spiked water is summarized in Table 3 and showed > 99 % recovery. These results prove that our N-CDs nanoprobe is excellent method for aniline sensing in real samples.

In order to determine whether the mechanism behind the observed fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by aniline is dynamic or static quenching or both of them, let us remember that the static quenching is mainly owing to the creation of a ground-state complex of the fluorophore and the quenchers. While the dynamic quenching involves excited-state interaction between both fluorophores and quenchers. As shown in Fig.6b, I0/I have a linear relation with the aniline concentration and thus indicating the predominance of a sole mechanism of the fluorescence quenching [27].As observed in Fig. 3b, the small shift in the absorption peak of aniline, in the presence of N-CDs, indicates the possible formation of a stable complex and thus suggests the N-CD fluorescence quenching mechanism to have astatic nature.

To confirm that the probable fluorescence quenching is initiated by the creation of a ground-state complex, we measured the differential absorption between the spectra of [N-CDs/Aniline] system and aniline alone at the same concentrations. The results are shown in Fig.4b/B, where curve B represents the difference of absorption spectra of [N-CDs/Aniline] system and aniline alone. As seen, the curve B is significantly different from the absorption spectrum of N-CDs, particularly at λ∼280 nm. These outcomes approve that the mechanism of N-CDs fluorescence quenching is essentially static and is initiated by the complex creation among N-CDs and aniline. A more direct manner to extricate the static quenching mechanism from that of the dynamic is to investigate the fluorescence lifetime behaviors of N-CDs in the absence and presence of aniline. The fluorescence decay of N-CDs with aniline concentrations is shown in Fig.10 . As seen, the decay curves show an exponential decay with two decay time constants, which can be fitted with equation
y = y
0
+
A 1
e
-
( X -
X 0
)
τ 1
+
A 2
e
-
( X -
X 0
)
τ 2
. The lifetimes of the as-synthesized N-CDs alone are τ1 = 1.5 ns and τ2 = 7.9 ns. The inset of Fig.10 shows the average lifetime of excitons in N-CDs in the absence and presence of aniline as calculated from the relation:
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

As seen, the excitonic average lifetime of our N-CDs shows no significant change with aniline. The latter firmly demonstrates that the fluorescence quenching procedure among the N-CDs and aniline has a static nature [27]. Other information about the static quenching mechanism can be extracted also from the data of the zeta potential of the synthesized N-CDs. Fig. 11 shows that the potential has a value of -16.7 mV. The latter implies that the surface of N-CDs has a negative charge because of the carboxyl functional group which seems dominating the surface of N-CDs [28]. For aniline surface, it is expected to be positively charged because of anilinium cations formation when aniline is water-diluted since aniline is well known to be a weak base (proton acceptor) while water is amphoteric and thus acts as a weak acid (proton donor).

Consequently, when the diluted aniline is added to N-CDs, carboxyl groups on the surface of the dots can be attached to anilinium. When the N-CDs are optically excited, anilinium behaves as an acceptor of electrons, eventually leading to N-CDs fluorescence quenching. The large surface area of our N-CDs, owing to the ultra-small size of the dots, make N-CDs efficient electron acceptors. Finally, as we confirmed the interaction between the N-CDs and aniline to be initiated by a static quenching, therefore the N-CDs fluorescence quenching can be investigated by the modified Stern–Volmer equation [27].
(2)
I 0
I 0
- I
=
1
f
K a
Q
+
1 f
where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence and absence of aniline, respectively; Ka is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant for the accessible fraction; f is the fraction of the initial fluorescence accessible to aniline, which designates the fractional fluorescence of a total fluorescence for interaction with aniline. As shown in Fig. 12 , the plot of I0/(I0 − I) versus 1/[Q] displayed a linear trend and yielded the values of f −1 as the intercept and (f Ka ) −1 is the slope. As f −1 represents the extrapolation to infinite aniline concentration, thus it represents the reciprocal of the fluorescence that was quenched by aniline as a quencher. From Fig.12, the value of f and Kα were found to be 0.11 and 6.7 × 106 M, respectively indicating that only 11 % of the initial fluorescence of N-CDs was accessible for quenching.

To conclude, we presented a highly sensitive fluorescent sensor of aniline liquid based on ultra-small sized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots. The N-CDs were derived from ultrasonic-processed folic acid at relatively low synthesis temperature of 100 °C. We found the operation of the sensor to follow the static fluorescence quenching of N-CDs owing to electrostatic interaction with aniline. The limit of detection of the sensor is 3.75 nM (0.332 ppb) which is the smallest value obtained for aniline detection by any means. Furthermore, a real sample analysis was investigated using the N-CDs nano-probe with real tap water and excellent results were obtained with 99.7–101 % recovery. Thus, the present work may help in the establishment of an easy, sensitive, selective, quantitative, and eco-friendly nano-sensing method for monitoring hazardous aniline against environmental contamination.

Y.M. Bakier: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology. M. Ghali: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. A. Elkun: Investigation, Data curation. A.M. Beltagi: Validation. W.K. Zahra: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The Authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT) through grant 4414, and Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF) through project 4926 and the Higher Education Ministry in Egypt.
